original_problem: In a small town, there are three switches, but they are not labelled. Each switch corresponds to one of three light bulbs in a room. Each light bulb is either on or off. You can turn the lights on and off as many times as you want, but you can only enter the room one time to observe the light bulbs. How can you figure out which switch corresponds to which light bulb? Additionally, one of the bulbs is special and will only turn on if the switch is flipped an odd number of times, while the other two bulbs will turn on regardless of how many times their switches are flipped. The special bulb is either the first, second, or third bulb, but you don't know which one. After figuring out the switches, you must determine how many total combinations of switch flips are possible to turn on exactly two of the three bulbs, considering the special bulb's constraint.
deconstructed_problem: In a small town, there are three switches, but they are not labelled. Each switch corresponds to one of three light bulbs in a room. Each light bulb is either on or off. You can turn the lights on and off as many times as you want, but you can only enter the room one time to observe the light bulbs. How can you figure out which switch corresponds to which light bulb? Additionally, one of the bulbs is special and will only turn on ଯଦି (if) the switch is flipped an odd number of times, while the other two bulbs will turn on regardless of how many times their switches are flipped. The special bulb is either the first, second, or third bulb, but you don't know which one. After figuring out the switches, you must determine how many ମୋଟ (total) combinations of switch flips are possible to turn on exactly two of the three bulbs, considering the special bulb's constraint.
original_steps: Step 1: Identify the special bulb by flipping the first switch on for 5 minutes, then off, then flipping the second switch on and immediately entering the room to observe the bulbs. If a bulb is on, it's not the special one. If a bulb is off but warm, it might be the special one because it was recently turned off.

Step 2: Determine the special bulb by using the process of elimination. If the first bulb is warm but off, flip the third switch on and off, then on again, and observe. If it turns on, it's the special bulb because it was flipped an odd number of times.
Step 3: After identifying the special bulb, flip the corresponding switch an odd number of times to turn it on, then flip another switch to turn on one of the regular bulbs.

Step 4: Calculate the number of ways to turn on exactly two bulbs considering the special bulb's constraint. If the special bulb is one of the two to be turned on, there are 2 options for which of the other two bulbs to turn on. For each of these scenarios, there are 2 possible sequences of flips for the special bulb (1 flip or 3 flips) and 2 possible sequences for the regular bulb (1 flip or 2 flips, but since we are considering turning it on, only 1 flip is relevant). However, the special bulb must be flipped an odd number of times, limiting combinations.

Step 5: Account for scenarios where the special bulb is not one of the two to be turned on. In this case, any combination of flips for the two regular bulbs that results in exactly two being on is valid, but the special bulb's switch must remain untouched or flipped an even number of times, which doesn't add valid combinations since we're focusing on turning bulbs on.
